([/ˈɡeɪnzbərə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); 14 May 1727 (baptised) – 2 August 1788) was an English [portrait](/wiki/Portrait "Portrait") and [landscape painter](/wiki/Landscape_painter "Landscape painter"), [draughtsman](/wiki/Drawing "Drawing"), and [printmaker](/wiki/Printmaker "Printmaker"). Along with his rival Sir [Joshua Reynolds](/wiki/Joshua_Reynolds "Joshua Reynolds"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) he is considered one of the most important British artists of the second half of the 18th century.[[2]](#cite_note-2) He painted quickly, and the works of his maturity are characterised by a light palette and easy strokes.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

English painter (1727–1788)

**Thomas Gainsborough** ([/ˈɡeɪnzbərə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); 14 May 1727 (baptised) – 2 August 1788) was an English [portrait](/wiki/Portrait "Portrait") and [landscape painter](/wiki/Landscape_painter "Landscape painter"), [draughtsman](/wiki/Drawing "Drawing"), and [printmaker](/wiki/Printmaker "Printmaker"). Along with his rival Sir [Joshua Reynolds](/wiki/Joshua_Reynolds "Joshua Reynolds"),[[1]](#cite_note-1) he is considered one of the most important British artists of the second half of the 18th century.[[2]](#cite_note-2) He painted quickly, and the works of his maturity are characterised by a light palette and easy strokes.

Despite being a prolific portrait painter, Gainsborough gained greater satisfaction from his landscapes.[[3]](#cite_note-3) He is credited (with [Richard Wilson](/wiki/Richard_Wilson_(painter) "Richard Wilson (painter)")) as the originator of the 18th-century British landscape school. Gainsborough was a founding member of the [Royal Academy](/wiki/Royal_Academy_of_Arts "Royal Academy of Arts").

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Gainsborough&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Thomas_Gainsborough_-_Lady_Lloyd_and_Her_Son%2C_Richard_Savage_Lloyd%2C_of_Hintlesham_Hall%2C_Suffolk_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/330px-Thomas_Gainsborough_-_Lady_Lloyd_and_Her_Son%2C_Richard_Savage_Lloyd%2C_of_Hintlesham_Hall%2C_Suffolk_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Gainsborough_-_Lady_Lloyd_and_Her_Son,_Richard_Savage_Lloyd,_of_Hintlesham_Hall,_Suffolk_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*Lady Lloyd and Her Son, Richard Savage Lloyd, of Hintlesham Hall, Suffolk* (1745–46), [Yale Center for British Art](/wiki/Yale_Center_for_British_Art "Yale Center for British Art")

Gainsborough was born in [Sudbury](/wiki/Sudbury,_Suffolk "Sudbury, Suffolk"), Suffolk, the youngest son of John Gainsborough, a weaver and maker of woollen goods, and his wife Mary, sister of the Reverend Humphry Burroughs.[[4]](#cite_note-NGA-4) One of Gainsborough's brothers, [Humphrey](/wiki/Humphrey_Gainsborough "Humphrey Gainsborough"), is said to have invented the method of condensing steam in a separate vessel, which was of great service to [James Watt](/wiki/James_Watt "James Watt"); another brother, John, was known as *Scheming Jack* because of his passion for designing curiosities.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The artist spent his childhood at what is now [Gainsborough's House](/wiki/Gainsborough%27s_House "Gainsborough's House"), on Gainsborough Street, Sudbury. He later resided there following the death of his father in 1748 and before his move to Ipswich.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The building is now a house-museum dedicated to his life and art.

As a boy he demonstrated impressive drawing and painting skills. At the age of ten he was painting heads and small landscapes, including a miniature self-portrait.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Gainsborough left home in 1740 to study art in London, where he trained under engraver [Hubert Gravelot](/wiki/Hubert-Fran%C3%A7ois_Gravelot "Hubert-François Gravelot")[[4]](#cite_note-NGA-4) but became associated with [William Hogarth](/wiki/William_Hogarth "William Hogarth") and his school. He assisted [Francis Hayman](/wiki/Francis_Hayman "Francis Hayman") in decorating the supper boxes at [Vauxhall Gardens](/wiki/Vauxhall_Gardens "Vauxhall Gardens").[[4]](#cite_note-NGA-4)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Gainsborough&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Suffolk

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Gainsborough&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Suffolk")]

In 1746, Gainsborough married Margaret Burr, an illegitimate daughter of the [3rd Duke of Beaufort](/wiki/Henry_Scudamore,_3rd_Duke_of_Beaufort "Henry Scudamore, 3rd Duke of Beaufort"), who had settled a £200 annuity on her. The artist's work, then mostly consisting of landscape paintings, was not selling well. He returned to Sudbury in 1748–1749 and concentrated on painting portraits.[[8]](#cite_note-MMA-8)
While still in Suffolk, Gainsborough painted a portrait of *The Rev. John Chafy Playing the Violoncello in a Landscape* (c. 1750–1752; [Tate](/wiki/Tate "Tate") Gallery, London).[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In 1752, he and his family, now including two daughters, [Mary](/wiki/Mary_Gainsborough "Mary Gainsborough") ("Molly", 1750–1826) and Margaret ("Peggy", 1751–1820),[[10]](#cite_note-JH2017-10) moved to Ipswich. Commissions for portraits increased, but his clients included mainly local merchants and squires. He had to borrow against his wife's annuity.[[8]](#cite_note-MMA-8) Toward the end of his time in Ipswich, he painted a self-portrait,[[11]](#cite_note-11) now in the permanent collection of the [National Portrait Gallery, London](/wiki/National_Portrait_Gallery,_London "National Portrait Gallery, London").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Bath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Gainsborough&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Bath")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Portrait_of_Ann_Ford_%28Gainsborough%29.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_Ann_Ford_%28Gainsborough%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_Ann_Ford_(Gainsborough).jpg)

*[Portrait of Ann Ford](/wiki/Portrait_of_Ann_Ford "Portrait of Ann Ford")*, 1760, [Cincinnati Art Museum](/wiki/Cincinnati_Art_Museum "Cincinnati Art Museum")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/The_Blue_Boy.jpg/250px-The_Blue_Boy.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Blue_Boy.jpg)

*[The Blue Boy](/wiki/The_Blue_Boy "The Blue Boy")* (1770). [Huntington Library](/wiki/Huntington_Library "Huntington Library"), San Marino, California

In 1759, Gainsborough and his family moved to [Bath](/wiki/Bath,_Somerset "Bath, Somerset"), living at number 17 [The Circus](/wiki/The_Circus,_Bath "The Circus, Bath").[[13]](#cite_note-13) There, he studied portraits by [van Dyck](/wiki/Anthony_van_Dyck "Anthony van Dyck") and was eventually able to attract a fashionable clientele. Beginning with the [Exhibition of 1761](/wiki/Exhibition_of_1761 "Exhibition of 1761") he sent work to the annual exhibition of the [Society of Artists of Great Britain](/wiki/Society_of_Artists_of_Great_Britain "Society of Artists of Great Britain") (of which he was one of the earliest members) at [Spring Gardens](/wiki/Spring_Gardens "Spring Gardens") in London. From 1769 he submitted works to the [Royal Academy](/wiki/Royal_Academy_of_Arts "Royal Academy of Arts")'s [annual exhibitions](/wiki/Summer_Exhibition "Summer Exhibition"). The exhibitions helped him enhance his reputation, and he was invited to become a founding member of the Royal Academy in 1769. His relationship with the academy was not an easy one and he stopped exhibiting his paintings in 1773.

Despite Gainsborough's increasing popularity and success in painting portraits for fashionable society, he expressed frustration during his Bath period at the demands of such work and that it prevented him from pursuing his preferred artistic interests. In a letter to a friend in the 1760s Gainsborough wrote: "I'm sick of Portraits and wish very much to take my [Viol](/wiki/Viol "Viol") da Gamba and walk off to some sweet Village where I can paint Landskips [landscapes] and enjoy the fag End of Life in quietness and ease".[[14]](#cite_note-14) Of the men he had to deal with as patrons and admirers, and their pretensions, he wrote:

> ... damn Gentlemen, there is not such a set of Enemies to a real artist in the world as they are, if not kept at a proper distance. They think ... that they reward your merit by their Company & notice; but I ... know that they have but one part worth looking at, and that is their Purse; their Hearts are seldom near enough the right place to get a sight of it.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Gainsborough was so keen a viol da gamba player that he had at this stage five of the instruments, three made by Henry Jaye and two by [Barak Norman](/wiki/Barak_Norman "Barak Norman").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### London

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Gainsborough&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: London")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Thomas_Gainsborough%2C_Frances_Browne%2C_Mrs_John_Douglas_%281746_-_1811%29%2C_1783-84_at_Waddesdon_Manor.jpg/250px-Thomas_Gainsborough%2C_Frances_Browne%2C_Mrs_John_Douglas_%281746_-_1811%29%2C_1783-84_at_Waddesdon_Manor.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Gainsborough,_Frances_Browne,_Mrs_John_Douglas_(1746_-_1811),_1783-84_at_Waddesdon_Manor.jpg)

*Frances Browne, Mrs John Douglas (1746–1811)*, 1783–84, [Waddesdon Manor](/wiki/Waddesdon_Manor "Waddesdon Manor")

In 1774, Gainsborough and his family moved to London to live in [Schomberg House](/wiki/Schomberg_House "Schomberg House"), Pall Mall.[[4]](#cite_note-NGA-4)[[17]](#cite_note-17) A commemorative [blue plaque](/wiki/Blue_plaque "Blue plaque") was put on the house in 1951.[[18]](#cite_note-18) In 1777, he again began to exhibit his paintings at the Royal Academy, including portraits of contemporary celebrities, such as the [Duke](/wiki/Prince_Henry,_Duke_of_Cumberland_and_Strathearn "Prince Henry, Duke of Cumberland and Strathearn") and [Duchess of Cumberland](/wiki/Anne_Horton "Anne Horton"). Exhibitions of his work continued for the next six years. About this time, Gainsborough began experimenting with [printmaking](/wiki/Printmaking "Printmaking") using the then-novel techniques of [aquatint](/wiki/Aquatint "Aquatint") and [soft-ground etching](/wiki/Soft-ground_etching "Soft-ground etching").[[19]](#cite_note-Rosenthal_Oxford-19)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Thomas_Gainsborough_%28English_-_Portrait_of_Anne%2C_Countess_of_Chesterfield_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Thomas_Gainsborough_%28English_-_Portrait_of_Anne%2C_Countess_of_Chesterfield_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Gainsborough_(English_-_Portrait_of_Anne,_Countess_of_Chesterfield_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*[Portrait of Anne, Countess of Chesterfield](/wiki/Portrait_of_Anne,_Countess_of_Chesterfield "Portrait of Anne, Countess of Chesterfield")* (1777–78), [J. Paul Getty Museum](/wiki/J._Paul_Getty_Museum "J. Paul Getty Museum"). His later pictures are characterised by a light palette and easy strokes.

During the 1770s and 1780s Gainsborough developed a type of portrait in which he integrated the sitter into the landscape. An example of this is his portrait of Frances Browne, Mrs John Douglas (1746–1811) which can be seen at [Waddesdon Manor](/wiki/Waddesdon_Manor "Waddesdon Manor"). The sitter has withdrawn to a secluded and overgrown corner of a garden to read a letter, her pose recalling the traditional representation of Melancholy. Gainsborough emphasised the relationship between Mrs Douglas and her environment by painting the clouds behind her and the drapery billowing across her lap with similar silvery violet tones and fluid brushstrokes. This portrait was included in his first private exhibition at Schomberg House in 1784.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

In 1776, Gainsborough painted a portrait of [Johann Christian Bach](/wiki/Johann_Christian_Bach "Johann Christian Bach"),[[21]](#cite_note-JCBach-21) the youngest son of [Johann Sebastian Bach](/wiki/Johann_Sebastian_Bach "Johann Sebastian Bach").[[22]](#cite_note-Burnett-22) Bach's former teacher [Padre Martini](/wiki/Padre_Martini "Padre Martini") of Bologna, Italy, was assembling a collection of portraits of musicians, and Bach asked Gainsborough to paint his portrait as part of this collection.[[21]](#cite_note-JCBach-21) The portrait now hangs in the [National Portrait Gallery, London](/wiki/National_Portrait_Gallery,_London "National Portrait Gallery, London").[[21]](#cite_note-JCBach-21)

In 1780, he painted the portraits of King [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III") and Queen [Charlotte](/wiki/Charlotte_of_Mecklenburg-Strelitz "Charlotte of Mecklenburg-Strelitz") and afterwards received other royal commissions. In February 1780, his daughter Molly was married to his musician friend [Johann Christian Fischer](/wiki/Johann_Christian_Fischer "Johann Christian Fischer"), to Gainsborough's dismay, as he realized that Fischer was forming an attachment to Molly while carrying on flirtation with Peggy.[[10]](#cite_note-JH2017-10) The marriage between Molly and Fischer lasted only eight months, owing to their discord and Fischer's deceit.[[10]](#cite_note-JH2017-10)

In 1784, Principal Painter in Ordinary [Allan Ramsay](/wiki/Allan_Ramsay_(artist) "Allan Ramsay (artist)") died and the King was obliged to give the job to Gainsborough's rival and Academy president, [Joshua Reynolds](/wiki/Joshua_Reynolds "Joshua Reynolds"). Gainsborough remained the royal family's favourite painter, however.

In his later years, Gainsborough often painted landscapes. With [Richard Wilson](/wiki/Richard_Wilson_(painter) "Richard Wilson (painter)"), he was one of the originators of the eighteenth-century British landscape school; though simultaneously, in conjunction with Reynolds, he was the dominant British portraitist of the second half of the 18th century.

William Jackson in his contemporary essays said of him "to his intimate friends he was sincere and honest and that his heart was always alive to every feeling of honour and generosity".[[23]](#cite_note-23) Gainsborough did not particularly enjoy reading but letters written to his friends were penned in such an exceptional conversational manner that the style could not be equalled.[[24]](#cite_note-24) As a letter writer [Henry Bate-Dudley](/wiki/Sir_Henry_Dudley,_1st_Baronet "Sir Henry Dudley, 1st Baronet") said of him "a selection of his letters would offer the world as much originality and beauty as is ever traced in his paintings".[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In the 1780s, Gainsborough used a device he called a "Showbox" to compose landscapes and display them backlit on glass. The original box is on display in the [Victoria and Albert Museum](/wiki/Victoria_and_Albert_Museum "Victoria and Albert Museum") with a reproduction transparency.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

He died of cancer on 2 August 1788 at the age of 61. According to his daughter Peggy, his last words were "[van Dyck](/wiki/Van_Dyck "Van Dyck")".[[27]](#cite_note-27) He is interred in the churchyard [St Anne's Church, Kew](/wiki/St_Anne%27s_Church,_Kew "St Anne's Church, Kew"), Surrey, (located on Kew Green). It was his express wish to be buried near his friend [Joshua Kirby](/wiki/Joshua_Kirby "Joshua Kirby"). Later his wife and nephew [Gainsborough Dupont](/wiki/Gainsborough_Dupont "Gainsborough Dupont") were interred with him. Coincidentally [Johan Zoffany](/wiki/Johan_Zoffany "Johan Zoffany") and [Franz Bauer](/wiki/Franz_Bauer "Franz Bauer") are also buried in the graveyard. In 2011, an appeal was given to pay the costs of restoration of his tomb, and the tomb was restored in 2012.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) A street in Kew, Gainsborough Road, is named after him.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

## Technique

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Gainsborough&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Technique")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Girl_with_Pigs_by_Thomas_Gainsborough.jpg/250px-Girl_with_Pigs_by_Thomas_Gainsborough.jpg)](/wiki/File:Girl_with_Pigs_by_Thomas_Gainsborough.jpg)

*Girl with Pigs*, 1781–82, private collection, was said by Sir Joshua Reynolds to be "the best picture he ever painted".[[31]](#cite_note-Willes-31)

The art historian [Michael Rosenthal](/wiki/Michael_Rosenthal "Michael Rosenthal") described Gainsborough as "one of the most technically proficient and, at the same time, most experimental artists of his time".[[19]](#cite_note-Rosenthal_Oxford-19) He was noted for the speed with which he applied paint, and he worked more from observations of nature (and of human nature) than from application of formal academic rules.[[19]](#cite_note-Rosenthal_Oxford-19) The poetic sensibility of his paintings caused [John Constable](/wiki/John_Constable "John Constable") to say, "On looking at them, we find tears in our eyes and know not what brings them."

Gainsborough's enthusiasm for landscapes is shown in the way he merged figures of the portraits with the scenes behind them. His landscapes were often painted at night by candlelight, using a tabletop arrangement of stones, pieces of mirrors, broccoli, and the like as a model.[[19]](#cite_note-Rosenthal_Oxford-19) His later work was characterised by a light palette and easy, economical strokes.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Gainsborough's only known assistant was his nephew, [Gainsborough Dupont](/wiki/Gainsborough_Dupont "Gainsborough Dupont").[[4]](#cite_note-NGA-4)

## Reputation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Gainsborough&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Reputation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Thomas_Gainsborough_-_The_Honourable_Mrs_Graham_%281757_-_1792%29_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg/250px-Thomas_Gainsborough_-_The_Honourable_Mrs_Graham_%281757_-_1792%29_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Gainsborough_-_The_Honourable_Mrs_Graham_(1757_-_1792)_-_Google_Art_Project.jpg)

*[Portrait of Mrs Mary Graham](/wiki/Portrait_of_Mrs_Mary_Graham "Portrait of Mrs Mary Graham")* (1777) [Scottish National Gallery](/wiki/Scottish_National_Gallery "Scottish National Gallery")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Thomas_Gainsborough_001.jpg/250px-Thomas_Gainsborough_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Gainsborough_001.jpg)

*[The Harvest Wagon](/wiki/The_Harvest_Wagon "The Harvest Wagon")* (1767)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Mrs._Ralph_Izard_%28Alice_De_Lancey%2C_1746-47%E2%80%931832%29_MET_DP162167.jpg/250px-Mrs._Ralph_Izard_%28Alice_De_Lancey%2C_1746-47%E2%80%931832%29_MET_DP162167.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mrs._Ralph_Izard_(Alice_De_Lancey,_1746-47%E2%80%931832)_MET_DP162167.jpg)

[Alice De Lancey Izard](/wiki/Alice_De_Lancey_Izard "Alice De Lancey Izard") (between 1747 and 1788) [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art")

His more famous works, *[The Blue Boy](/wiki/The_Blue_Boy "The Blue Boy")*; *[Mr and Mrs Andrews](/wiki/Mr_and_Mrs_Andrews "Mr and Mrs Andrews")*; *[Portrait of Mrs Mary Graham](/wiki/Portrait_of_Mrs_Mary_Graham "Portrait of Mrs Mary Graham")*; *Mary and Margaret: The Painter's Daughters*; *William Hallett and His Wife Elizabeth, nee Stephen*, known as *[The Morning Walk](/wiki/Mr_and_Mrs_William_Hallett "Mr and Mrs William Hallett")*; and *Cottage Girl with Dog and Pitcher*, display the unique individuality of his subjects.
His rival, [Joshua Reynolds](/wiki/Joshua_Reynolds "Joshua Reynolds") wrote that the painting *Girl with Pigs* was "the best picture he (Gainsborough) ever painted or perhaps ever will".[[31]](#cite_note-Willes-31)

Gainsborough's works became popular with collectors from the 1850s on, after [Lionel de Rothschild](/wiki/Lionel_de_Rothschild "Lionel de Rothschild") began buying his portraits. The rapid rise in the value of pictures by Gainsborough and also by Reynolds in the mid 19th century was partly because the Rothschild family, including [Ferdinand de Rothschild](/wiki/Ferdinand_de_Rothschild "Ferdinand de Rothschild") began collecting them.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

In 2011, Gainsborough's portrait of *Miss Read* (Mrs Frances Villebois) was sold by [Michael Pearson, 4th Viscount Cowdray](/wiki/Michael_Pearson,_4th_Viscount_Cowdray "Michael Pearson, 4th Viscount Cowdray"), for a record price of £6.54M, at Christie's in London.[[34]](#cite_note-34) She was a matrilineal descendant of [Cecily Neville, Duchess of York](/wiki/Cecily_Neville,_Duchess_of_York "Cecily Neville, Duchess of York").[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-Fig1-36)

## Popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Thomas_Gainsborough&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Popular culture")]

* Gainsborough's portrait *[The Blue Boy](/wiki/The_Blue_Boy "The Blue Boy")* is shown in the 1988 comedy movie *[The Naked Gun: From the Files of Police Squad!](/wiki/The_Naked_Gun:_From_the_Files_of_Police_Squad! "The Naked Gun: From the Files of Police Squad!")* in the office of the antagonist, Vincent Ludwig.
* Gainsborough's *[The Blue Boy](/wiki/The_Blue_Boy "The Blue Boy")* is also referenced in a 1995 episode of *[Keeping Up Appearances](/wiki/Keeping_Up_Appearances "Keeping Up Appearances")* with Hyacinth Bucket stating at country house estate sale that she was "looking for something like Gainsborough's Blue Boy...only cheaper...".
* [Cecil Beaton](/wiki/Cecil_Beaton "Cecil Beaton")'s play *Gainsborough's Girls* is set in London in 1774 when the painter moved his family to the capital. Previously unpublished, it received its first performance in [Sudbury, Suffolk](/wiki/Sudbury,_Suffolk "Sudbury, Suffolk") in 2019, followed by a short run at the Tower Theatre, London.[[37]](#cite_note-37)
* [Simon Edge](/wiki/Simon_Edge "Simon Edge")'s comic novel *A Right Royal Face-Off* focuses on Gainsborough's relationship with King George III and his family, and his rivalry with [Joshua Reynolds](/wiki/Joshua_Reynolds "Joshua Reynolds").[[38]](#cite_note-38)
* [Stanley Kubrick](/wiki/Stanley_Kubrick "Stanley Kubrick") was inspired by Gainsborough's paintings, amongst other artists of the 18th century, in creating the look and mannerisms for his 1975 film [Barry Lyndon](/wiki/Barry_Lyndon "Barry Lyndon").[[39]](#cite_note-39)
* Gainsborough's portrait *[The Morning Walk (Portrait of Mr and Mrs William Hallett)](/wiki/Mr_and_Mrs_William_Hallett "Mr and Mrs William Hallett")* is clearly visible over actor [Daniel Craig](/wiki/Daniel_Craig "Daniel Craig")'s shoulder during a scene in the 2012 [James Bond](/wiki/James_Bond "James Bond") film *[Skyfall](/wiki/Skyfall "Skyfall")* set in the [National Gallery](/wiki/National_Gallery "National Gallery").[[40]](#cite_note-40)
* Gainsborough (played by [Cecil Kellaway](/wiki/Cecil_Kellaway "Cecil Kellaway")) performs a vital role in the 1945 film *[Kitty](/wiki/Kitty_(1945_film) "Kitty (1945 film)")*; saving the eponymous heroine from prison sets the plot, based on [Pygmalion](/wiki/Pygmalion_(play) "Pygmalion (play)"), in motion.
* Gainsborough's portrait *The Gravenor Family* was cut and pasted into the sleeve of the 1983 [Electric Light Orchestra](/wiki/Electric_Light_Orchestra "Electric Light Orchestra") album *[Secret Messages](/wiki/Secret_Messages "Secret Messages")*.
* Gainsborough's portrait *[Cornard Wood, near Sudbury, Suffolk](/wiki/Cornard_Wood,_near_Sudbury,_Suffolk "Cornard Wood, near Sudbury, Suffolk")* is referenced in the first season of the 2024 [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") show [The Gentlemen](/wiki/The_Gentlemen_(2024_TV_series) "The Gentlemen (2024 TV series)").
* The *[CSS extended color](/wiki/Web_colors#extended_colors "Web colors")* Gainsboro (hex value `#DCDCDC`) is attributed to *[Gainsborough's use of similar light tones](/wiki/Shades_of_gray#Gainsboro "Shades of gray")*.
